retrieved_docs.title: Normal Tension Glaucoma -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Conjunctival hyperemia and ocular surface symptoms are the most common side effects seen with all classes of topical IOP-lowering medications. Side effects of prostaglandin analogs include hypertrichosis and changes in iris pigmentation. Prostaglandin-associated periorbitopathy is an important complication of long-term prostaglandin analog use. This includes orbital fat atrophy, ptosis, chronic inflammation of the eyelids, and skin discoloration. It is seen most commonly with bimatoprost. [56] [57] In addition, filtration surgery may have lower success rates in these patients. Topical beta-blockers are relatively contraindicated in patients with asthma and COPD. Additional systemic side effects include cardiovascular effects, vivid dreams, erectile dysfunction, and mood changes. Carbonic anhydrase inhibitors should be used with caution in those with a history of sulfonamide allergy. Alpha-agonists are contraindicated in young children. Complications from SLT are rare but may include intraocular inflammation or an IOP spike after treatment. Glaucoma surgery complications include failure, hypotony, cataracts, phlebitis, tube erosion, endophthalmitis, and corneal decompensation.

retrieved_docs.title: Pigment Dispersion Syndrome -- Treatment / Management -- Other Antiglaucoma Medications
retrieved_docs.content: Prostaglandin analogs, beta-adrenergic blockers, and alpha agonists are considered safe in PG. Mastropasqua, in their randomized trial, showed that latanoprost is more effective than timolol in controlling IOP in PG patients. But prostaglandins don't affect pigment liberation. A combination of medical management and a laser is beneficial in many cases. After prostaglandins, the following drugs are b-adrenergic antagonists, carbonic anhydrase inhibitors, and alpha-adrenergic drugs. [46]

retrieved_docs.title: Open Angle Glaucoma -- Toxicokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Pseudoexfoliation syndrome: Deposits of white dandruff-like material on the anterior lens surface, iris, and anterior chamber structures. [61]

retrieved_docs.title: Neovascular Glaucoma -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Patients coming from geographic locations or ethnicities with significant rates of sickle cell disease and other hemoglobinopathies need to be evaluated regarding the possibility of sickle cell anemia, prior blood transfusions, and pain in the extremities.

retrieved_docs.title: Prostaglandins -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: In glaucoma, intraocular pressure should be monitored to assess the efficacy of latanoprost, travoprost, bimatoprost, tafluprost, and latanoprostene bunod. [38] For safety, monitoring should include conjunctival hyperemia, iris pigmentation changes, eyelash alterations, and ocular discomfort. [38]

retrieved_docs.title: Lens-Induced Glaucoma -- Evaluation -- Clinical Examination
retrieved_docs.content: Ocular adnexa: Possible swelling and redness of the eyelids

retrieved_docs.title: Posterior Polymorphous Corneal Dystrophy -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: If findings substantiate a diagnosis of glaucoma, then initiate first-line topical glaucoma therapy. Monotherapy with a prostaglandin analog will control intraocular pressure (IOP) in the majority of cases. Changing class or adding a medication may be necessary to realize IOP control. Laser peripheral iridotomy (LPI) is not recommended if epithelialization of the trabecular meshwork has been identified. If unable to achieve or maintain rigid IOP control, glaucoma filtering surgery may be necessary. Minimally invasive glaucoma surgery (MIGS) offers promise.

retrieved_docs.title: Pigment Dispersion Syndrome -- Complications -- Medical Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Corneal edema Non-resolving uveitis Angle-closure glaucoma Iris atrophy Iridoplegia Relative afferent pupillary defect Intractable glaucoma Post SLT IOP elevation

retrieved_docs.title: Uveitis -- Differential Diagnosis -- Most common causes of a red, painful eye:
retrieved_docs.content: Anterior uveitis Keratitis Corneal abrasion Acute angle closure glaucoma Conjunctivitis Scleritis

retrieved_docs.title: Pigment Dispersion Glaucoma -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: PDS patients at risk of converting to PG are advised to undergo periodic ophthalmologic examinations to monitor IOP and optic disc cupping. Similar to other forms of open-angle glaucoma, treatment is indicated for elevated IOP in the presence of functional and structural glaucomatous damage. Medical therapy, which ranges from beta-blockers, carbonic anhydrase inhibitors, prostaglandin analogs, or a combination of these, are the first-line treatment option in PG. Eye drop of pilocarpine is theoretically an ideal option for these patients considering that it reduces pupil movement, induces miosis, and limits posterior iris bowing. This therapy, however, is poorly tolerated considering the side effects including blurred vision, accommodative spasms, headaches, and retinal detachment. A randomized study found that latanoprost once daily was more effective in reducing IOP at one year and well-tolerated in patients with PG compared to timolol twice daily. [51]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Angle-Closure Glaucoma -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Numerous conditions can lead to elevated IOP, corneal haze, inflammation of the conjunctiva and the anterior segment, and similar signs and symptoms seen in patients with AAC. When evaluating a patient presenting with these manifestations, the following differential diagnosis should be considered: Allergic conjunctivitis Bacterial conjunctivitis (pink eye) Viral conjunctivitis Keratitis Episcleritis or scleritis Eye trauma Chemical injury Corneal ulcer Open-angle glaucoma Drug-induced glaucoma Malignant glaucoma Neovascular glaucoma Phacomorphic glaucoma Senile cataract (age-related cataract) Lens subluxation [50] Migraine headache [51] Cluster headache Suprachoroidal hemorrhage

retrieved_docs.title: Pigment Dispersion Syndrome -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: The patient of PDS or PG presenting to the clinic should be evaluated in detail by the ophthalmologists, and all the findings must be documented in detail. Patients with PG requiring treatment must be referred to a glaucoma specialist for targeted investigation. These include applanation tonometry, gonioscopy, visual field assessment, optical coherence tomography, and the need for antiglaucoma therapy or filtration surgery. Patients with complicated cataracts may need an expert opinion from a cataract surgeon or a uvea specialist. Those developing retinal complications like retinal holes, tears, or detachment may require intervention from a retina specialist. [58]

retrieved_docs.title: Diseases of the Eyelashes -- Etiology -- Abnormal Darkening of Lashes
retrieved_docs.content: Abnormal darkening of eyelashes may be seen in Diabetes type II and as a side effect of prostaglandin F2 alpha agents (which increase melanin production and melanocytes). Malformed Hair/ Brittle Hair

retrieved_docs.title: Open Angle Glaucoma -- Treatment / Management -- Topical medications
retrieved_docs.content: Prostaglandin analogs: Reduces IOP 25% to 33% by increasing the uveoscleral outflow of the aqueous humor (AH). [93] The usual dose is daily. Side effects include lengthening of eyelashes, pigmentation of lids or iris, exacerbation of uveitis or herpetic infection, and cystoid macular edema; it is preferred as initial therapy. The types of prostaglandin analogs used in routine clinics include: Latanoprost Travoprost Bimatoprost Tafluprost Latanoprostene Bunod: This has nitric oxide-donating properties.

retrieved_docs.title: Dry Eye Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Topical medications: Including glaucoma drops or preservative toxicity from eye drops containing preservatives [17] [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Prostaglandins -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Topical ophthalmic prostaglandin analogs, including latanoprost, bimatoprost, travoprost, tafluprost, and latanoprostene bunod, generally have limited systemic adverse effects. Headaches or migraines may occur but are typically reversible upon drug discontinuation. [32] Topical latanoprost may also cause ocular adverse effects over time, such as iris darkening, eyelash thickening, periocular skin pigmentation, and conjunctival hyperemia. [33] Bimatoprost is associated with increased eyelash prominence. [23] Misoprostol may cause nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain, chills, shivering, and fever. [14] Carboprost may cause nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and bronchospasm. [6] Dinoprostone is generally well tolerated but may carry a risk of uterine hyperstimulation due to its mechanism of action. [8] Alprostadil may cause transient penile pain and urethral bleeding. [34] [27]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 2189
retrieved_docs.content: Latanoprost, tafluprost, travoprost and unoprostone are all members of the class of prostaglandin analogues, which are antiglaucoma drugs and miotics. This therapeutic group is used to lower intra‑ocular pressure in patients with glaucoma by increasing aqueous humor outflow, thereby helping to preserve visual function. Recognizing that these four agents belong to the same pharmacologic category aids clinicians in selecting appropriate prostaglandin‑based therapy and understanding their shared mechanism of action and potential side‑effect profile.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2607
retrieved_docs.content: Prostaglandin Analog [EPC] is linked to the therapeutic class identified as “Prostaglandin analogues, antiglaucoma drugs and miotics,” a group that encompasses several specific prostaglandin‑derived agents used to lower intra‑ocular pressure. Within this class, latanoprost, travoprost and unoprostone are recognized members, each functioning as a prostaglandin analogue that helps manage glaucoma by enhancing aqueous humor outflow. The relationship between Prostaglandin Analog [EPC] and the broader category underscores its relevance to the same pharmacologic mechanisms and clinical applications shared by latanoprost, travoprost and unoprostone in the treatment of glaucomatous disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1946
retrieved_docs.content: Prostaglandin analogues, antiglaucoma drugs and miotics constitute a pharmacologic class used to treat glaucoma by enhancing uveoscleral outflow and lowering intra‑ocular pressure. Within this class, bimatoprost, tafluprost, travoprost and unoprostone are recognized members, each acting as a prostaglandin analogue with similar therapeutic objectives. The product Prostaglandin Analog [EPC] is associated with this class, reflecting its role as a formulation that contains or represents a prostaglandin analogue used in glaucoma management. Recognizing that these agents belong to the same therapeutic group aids clinicians in selecting appropriate therapy and anticipating class‑related effects and side‑effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 2168
retrieved_docs.content: Prostaglandin analogues, antiglaucoma drugs and miotics is a pharmacologic class that includes bimatoprost, latanoprost, travoprost, and unoprostone. These agents are prostaglandin analogues used in the treatment of glaucoma and ocular hypertension, working primarily by increasing uveoscleral outflow to lower intra‑ocular pressure. As members of the same drug class, they share similar mechanisms of action and therapeutic indications, and are often considered together when selecting antiglaucoma therapy for patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1306
retrieved_docs.content: Latanoprost, tafluprost and unoprostone are all classified as members of the group of prostaglandin analogues, antiglaucoma drugs and miotics. This pharmacologic class is used in the management of glaucoma because its agents lower intra‑ocular pressure by enhancing aqueous humor outflow through the uveoscleral pathway. By sharing the same mechanistic category, these three drugs provide clinicians with multiple therapeutic options within the prostaglandin analogue family for controlling intra‑ocular pressure and preserving visual function in patients with glaucoma.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1514
retrieved_docs.content: Bimatoprost, latanoprost, tafluprost, and unoprostone are all members of the prostaglandin analogues, antiglaucoma drugs and miotics class, a group of ocular medications used to lower intra‑ocular pressure in the treatment of glaucoma.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2320
retrieved_docs.content: Prostaglandin analogues, antiglaucoma drugs and miotics is a pharmacologic class that includes bimatoprost, latanoprost, tafluprost, travoprost and unoprostone. These agents are prostaglandin analogues used as antiglaucoma medications and miotics to lower intra‑ocular pressure, primarily by increasing uveoscleral outflow. Their shared classification highlights a common mechanism of action and underscores their central role in the therapeutic management of glaucoma.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 93
retrieved_docs.content: work by enhancing cholinergic activity, beta‑adrenergic blockers (e.g., befunolol, levobunolol) reduce aqueous production, and prostaglandin receptor agonists (e.g., omidenepag, omidenepag isopropyl) increase uveoscleral outflow. Understanding these relationships aids clinicians in selecting appropriate antiglaucoma regimens and in appreciating how different drug classes complement each other within the comprehensive management of glaucoma.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 41
retrieved_docs.content: Prostaglandins constitute a major subclass of eicosanoids and are central to a variety of physiological and pharmacological contexts. Measurement of prostaglandins includes the compounds themselves as essential components, reflecting their clinical relevance. In therapeutic classification, prostaglandins serve as the parent group for drug categories used in obstetrics, gynecology, and urinary‑tract disorders, as well as for broader groupings such as hormones, vitamins, and receptor sites. The enzyme cyclooxygenase (cyklooxygenasy) is directly linked to prostaglandin biosynthesis, underscoring the biochemical pathway that generates these mediators. A distinct pharmacological family—prostaglandin analogues, antiglaucoma drugs, and miotics—derives its structural basis from prostaglandins. Within this family, agents such as bimatoprost, latanoprost, latanoprostene bunod (a synthetic prostaglandin analogue), tafluprost, travoprost, unoprostone, and isopropyl unoprostone are recognized as members. These compounds share a common prostaglandin‑derived structural class, which confers ocular hypotensive activity useful in the management of glaucoma. By grouping these agents under the prostaglandin analogue umbrella, clinicians can appreciate their shared mechanism of increasing aqueous‑humor outflow while also recognizing individual differences in potency, dosing, and side‑effect profiles. This integrated understanding of prostaglandin biology, its enzymatic synthesis, and its therapeutic analogues supports more informed patient care across multiple specialties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2200
retrieved_docs.content: Prostaglandin Analog [EPC] is linked to the therapeutic class described as “Prostaglandin analogues, antiglaucoma drugs and miotics.” This class comprises several specific agents that are used to manage glaucoma by reducing intra‑ocular pressure, namely bimatoprost, latanoprost, and unoprostone. All three drugs function as prostaglandin analogues and are categorized together with other antiglaucoma medications and miotics, reflecting their shared mechanism of enhancing aqueous humor outflow through the uveoscleral pathway. Recognizing that Prostaglandin Analog [EPC] is related to this group helps clinicians understand its role within the broader spectrum of glaucoma therapy and facilitates appropriate selection and combination of intra‑ocular pressure‑lowering agents for optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1738
retrieved_docs.content: Prostaglandin Analog [EPC] is linked to the therapeutic class described as “Prostaglandin analogues, antiglaucoma drugs and miotics,” a group of ocular medications that lower intra‑ocular pressure by enhancing aqueous humor outflow. This class comprises several specific agents, including bimatoprost, travoprost, and unoprostone, each of which functions as a prostaglandin analogue used in the management of glaucoma and ocular hypertension. Recognizing that Prostaglandin Analog [EPC] is associated with this broader category helps clinicians understand its role within the spectrum of antiglaucoma therapies and facilitates appropriate selection and combination of these agents for optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 1382
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroid ophthalmic antiinflammatory agents, plain constitute a subclass of ophthalmologic antiinflammatory agents, and the drug medrysone is an example of a plain corticosteroid formulated for ophthalmic anti‑inflammatory use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2265
retrieved_docs.content: Prostaglandin Analog [EPC] is linked to the therapeutic class “Prostaglandin analogues, antiglaucoma drugs and miotics,” a group that comprises the agents bimatoprost, latanoprost, travoprost and unoprostone. All four medications are prostaglandin analogues used in the management of glaucoma; they function as antiglaucoma drugs and possess miotic properties that help lower intra‑ocular pressure by enhancing uveoscleral outflow. Recognizing that Prostaglandin Analog [EPC] is associated with this class underscores its relevance to the same pharmacologic pathway and clinical application in glaucoma therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2128
retrieved_docs.content: Beta blocking agents, antiglaucoma preparations and miotics constitute a pharmacologic class that includes the drugs betaxolol and metipranolol. Both betaxolol and metipranolol are beta‑adrenergic antagonists that are employed as antiglaucoma agents and miotics, helping to lower intraocular pressure by decreasing aqueous humor production. As members of this same therapeutic group, they share similar mechanisms of action and are used in the management of ocular hypertension and glaucoma.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1347
retrieved_docs.content: Other antiglaucoma preparations in ATC is classified as a type of antiglaucoma preparations and miotics, indicating that it belongs to the broader group of medications used to lower intra‑ocular pressure and induce miosis in the management of glaucoma. Within this category, specific agents such as guanethidine and netarsudil are included as members, reflecting their role as distinct drug substances that share the therapeutic purpose of the class. Recognizing that guanethidine and netarsudil are part of the “Other antiglaucoma preparations in ATC” group helps clinicians understand the range of pharmacologic options available for glaucoma treatment and facilitates appropriate selection and categorization of these agents in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 572
retrieved_docs.content: Prostaglandins are a subclass of eicosanoids that are synthesized by the cyclooxygenase (COX) enzyme pathway; COX activity therefore has a direct relationship with prostaglandin production. The non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory drug celecoxib inhibits cyclooxygenase, altering the gene product affected by this chemical and consequently modulating prostaglandin levels. Within pharmacology, several drug groups are organized under prostaglandins as a parent category, including formulations used in obstetrics, gynaecology and urinary‑tract disorders as well as broader classifications such as “Hormones, Vitamins and Receptor Sites.” Prostaglandin analogues constitute an important therapeutic class, particularly in ophthalmology where they function as antiglaucoma agents and miotics. The group “Prostaglandin analogues, antiglaucoma drugs and miotics” comprises bimatoprost, latanoprost, latanoprostene bunod, tafluprost, and unoprostone (including its isopropyl derivative). Each of these compounds shares a structural class based on prostaglandins, reflecting their design to mimic natural prostaglandin activity while providing enhanced ocular efficacy. Latanoprostene bunod is specifically identified as a synthetic prostaglandin analogue. Overall, the interconnectedness of prostaglandins with eicosanoid biology, cyclooxygenase enzymology, and a range of clinically relevant drug families underscores their central role in both physiological signaling and targeted therapeutic strategies, especially for the management of glaucoma and related ocular conditions.
